BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wind slabs represent the main danger.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The wind slabs of last week represent the main danger. As a consequence of new snow and strong wind the avalanche prone locations will become more prevalent. They can be released very easily. These have formed especially in gullies and bowls, and behind abrupt changes in the terrain. Weak layers exist in the old snowpack on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes. Avalanches can be triggered in the old snowpack reach large size in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.
Snow sport activities outside marked and open pistes call for experience and great restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The small quantity of fresh snow of the last few days and in particular the wind slabs formed by the strong wind are lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack.
Individual weak layers exist in the old snowpack especially on shady slopes. Avalanches can in some places be released in deep layers in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.
Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise as the day progresses to moistening of the snowpack in particular on sunny slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wind slabs and wet snow represent the main danger. Afternoon: As a consequence of the increasingly strong westerly wind the natural avalanche activity will quickly increase.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation moist and wet avalanches are possible as the day progresses, in particular medium-sized ones, caution is to be exercised on steep grassy slopes at low and intermediate altitudes, as well as at the base of rock walls.

Afternoon: Some snow will fall in particular along the border with France and along the border with Switzerland. As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures, snowfall and the moderate to strong westerly wind, fresh snow drift accumulations will form.
In gullies and bowls, and behind abrupt changes in the terrain slab avalanches are possible, even quite large ones. Avalanches can be released, even by a single winter sport participant or triggered naturally. This applies in particular on steep shady slopes at intermediate and high altitudes.
Backcountry touring calls for caution and restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
In the early morning the weather will be mild. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise in the late morning to gradual moistening of the snowpack in particular at low and intermediate altitudes.
Afternoon: As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures and the moderate to strong westerly wind, fresh snow drift accumulations will form during the next few days.
In particular intermediate altitudes and steep sunny slopes: The snowpack is largely stable and its surface has a crust that is strong in many cases.
High altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Wind slabs are lying on the smooth surface of an old snowpack.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be cold. In some localities increase in avalanche danger as a consequence of the strong to storm force northwesterly wind. By Friday further wind slabs will form in gullies and bowls and behind abrupt changes in the terrain as well as at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions. Along the border with France and along the border with Switzerland the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent and the danger is greater.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wind slabs and wet snow represent the main danger. Afternoon: As a consequence of the increasingly strong westerly wind the natural avalanche activity will quickly increase.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation moist and wet avalanches are possible as the day progresses, in particular medium-sized ones, caution is to be exercised on steep grassy slopes at low and intermediate altitudes, as well as at the base of rock walls.

Afternoon: Some snow will fall in particular along the border with France and along the border with Switzerland. As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures, snowfall and the moderate to strong westerly wind, fresh snow drift accumulations will form.
In gullies and bowls, and behind abrupt changes in the terrain slab avalanches are possible, even quite large ones. Avalanches can be released, even by a single winter sport participant or triggered naturally. This applies in particular on steep shady slopes at intermediate and high altitudes.
Backcountry touring calls for caution and restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
In the early morning the weather will be mild. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise in the late morning to gradual moistening of the snowpack in particular at low and intermediate altitudes.
Afternoon: As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures and the moderate to strong westerly wind, fresh snow drift accumulations will form during the next few days.
In particular intermediate altitudes and steep sunny slopes: The snowpack is largely stable and its surface has a crust that is strong in many cases.
High altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Wind slabs are lying on the smooth surface of an old snowpack.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be cold. In some localities increase in avalanche danger as a consequence of the strong to storm force northwesterly wind. By Friday further wind slabs will form in gullies and bowls and behind abrupt changes in the terrain as well as at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions. Along the border with France and along the border with Switzerland the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent and the danger is greater.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: In some places relatively hard layers of snow are lying on a moist old snowpack. In some cases the wind slabs have bonded still only poorly. Below approximately 1800 m medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large wet and gliding avalanches are possible.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: Weak layers in the upper part of the snowpack can still be released in some places in all aspects. This applies in particular on wind-loaded slopes above approximately 1800 m in all aspects. In particular on steep slopes avalanches can be triggered in the various wind slab layers and reach medium size. Weak layers in the upper part of the snowpack are difficult to recognise. As a consequence of warming during the day individual medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large wet avalanches are possible as the day progresses below approximately 1800 m.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: Towards its surface, the snowpack is hard. Towards its base, the snowpack is moist. The meteorological conditions will foster a gradual strengthening of the weak layers in all aspects.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Gradual increase in danger of dry avalanches as a consequence of the strong wind.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Late morning: Especially adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls: The fresh snow of Sunday as well as the sometimes deep wind slabs can be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies in particular on very steep slopes. In many cases they are medium-sized.
In particular in steep rocky terrain and on sunny slopes individual mostly small wet avalanches are possible as a consequence of solar radiation. Afternoon: As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures and the strong to storm force northwesterly wind, a treacherous avalanche situation will develop during the next two days. These conditions will bring about a gradual rise in the danger of dry avalanches from midday. Adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas and at a distance from ridgelines medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large dry slab avalanches are possible.
Backcountry touring calls for caution and restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
2 to 5 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall on Wednesday in all altitude zones. Towards its surface, the snowpack is soft and has a loosely bonded surface. The wind will transport the fresh and old snow.
10 to 30 cm of snow, and up to 50 cm in some localities, fell on Sunday. Especially in the areas bordering Piedmont. Where it is gradually settling down. The wind was light to moderate adjacent to ridgelines.
In some places new snow is lying on the soft surface of an old snowpack. Or on crusts.
Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes.
Especially adjacent to ridgelines in all aspects: Over a wide area snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be cold. Over a wide area storm force northerly wind: The avalanche danger will increase but remain within the current danger level. Above the tree line the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Gradual increase in danger of dry avalanches as a consequence of the strong wind.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Late morning: Especially adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls: The fresh snow of Sunday as well as the sometimes deep wind slabs can be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies in particular on very steep slopes. In many cases they are medium-sized.
In particular in steep rocky terrain and on sunny slopes individual mostly small wet avalanches are possible as a consequence of solar radiation. Afternoon: As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures and the strong to storm force northwesterly wind, a treacherous avalanche situation will develop during the next two days. These conditions will bring about a gradual rise in the danger of dry avalanches from midday. Adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas and at a distance from ridgelines medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large dry slab avalanches are possible.
Backcountry touring calls for caution and restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
2 to 5 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall on Wednesday in all altitude zones. Towards its surface, the snowpack is soft and has a loosely bonded surface. The wind will transport the fresh and old snow.
10 to 30 cm of snow, and up to 50 cm in some localities, fell on Sunday. Especially in the areas bordering Piedmont. Where it is gradually settling down. The wind was light to moderate adjacent to ridgelines.
In some places new snow is lying on the soft surface of an old snowpack. Or on crusts.
Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes.
Especially adjacent to ridgelines in all aspects: Over a wide area snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be cold. Over a wide area storm force northerly wind: The avalanche danger will increase but remain within the current danger level. Above the tree line the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Significant increase in danger of dry avalanches as a consequence of new snow and stormy weather.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Late morning: Especially adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls: The fresh snow of Sunday as well as the sometimes deep wind slabs can be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies in particular on very steep slopes. In isolated cases they are medium-sized.
In particular in steep rocky terrain and on sunny slopes individual mostly small wet avalanches are possible as a consequence of solar radiation.
Afternoon: As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures and the strong to storm force northwesterly wind, a treacherous avalanche situation will develop. The prevalence of the avalanche prone locations will increase. These conditions will bring about a rise in the danger of dry avalanches from midday. Adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas and at a distance from ridgelines medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large dry slab avalanches are possible.
Backcountry touring calls for caution and restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
10 to 30 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall on Wednesday in all altitude zones. Towards its surface, the snowpack is soft and has a loosely bonded surface. The wind will transport the new snow significantly.
10 to 30 cm of snow fell on Sunday. Especially in the areas bordering Piedmont. Where it is gradually settling down. The wind was light to moderate adjacent to ridgelines.
In some places new snow is lying on the soft surface of an old snowpack. Or on crusts.
Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes.
Especially adjacent to ridgelines in all aspects: Over a wide area snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be cold. Over a wide area storm force northerly wind: The avalanche danger will increase but remain within the current danger level. Along the border with France the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent and the danger is greater.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Significant increase in danger of dry avalanches as a consequence of new snow and stormy weather.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Late morning: Especially adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls: The fresh snow of Sunday as well as the sometimes deep wind slabs can be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies in particular on very steep slopes. In isolated cases they are medium-sized.
In particular in steep rocky terrain and on sunny slopes individual mostly small wet avalanches are possible as a consequence of solar radiation.
Afternoon: As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures and the strong to storm force northwesterly wind, a treacherous avalanche situation will develop. The prevalence of the avalanche prone locations will increase. These conditions will bring about a rise in the danger of dry avalanches from midday. Adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas and at a distance from ridgelines medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large dry slab avalanches are possible.
Backcountry touring calls for caution and restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
10 to 30 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall on Wednesday in all altitude zones. Towards its surface, the snowpack is soft and has a loosely bonded surface. The wind will transport the new snow significantly.
10 to 30 cm of snow fell on Sunday. Especially in the areas bordering Piedmont. Where it is gradually settling down. The wind was light to moderate adjacent to ridgelines.
In some places new snow is lying on the soft surface of an old snowpack. Or on crusts.
Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes.
Especially adjacent to ridgelines in all aspects: Over a wide area snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be cold. Over a wide area storm force northerly wind: The avalanche danger will increase but remain within the current danger level. Along the border with France the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent and the danger is greater.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Moist and wet avalanches are possible during the day. Slab avalanches require caution, in the regions exposed to a lot of wind in particular in the afternoon.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation moist and wet avalanches are possible as the day progresses, in particular medium-sized ones.
As a consequence of a moderate to strong westerly wind, clearly visible wind slabs will form in the afternoon in particular adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas as well as at elevated altitudes. In particular on steep shady slopes avalanches can be released in the various wind slab layers and reach large size in isolated cases. This applies in particular at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions.
Wind slabs can in isolated cases be released, even by a single winter sport participant.

Backcountry touring calls for careful route selection.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
In the early morning the weather will be very mild. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise in the late morning to significant moistening of the snowpack in particular at low and intermediate altitudes.
Afternoon: As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures and the moderate to strong westerly wind, fresh snow drift accumulations will form during the next few days.
In particular intermediate altitudes and steep sunny slopes: The new snow is lying on top of a quite favourable old snowpack. The snowpack is largely stable and its surface has a crust that is strong in many cases.

High altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Wind slabs are lying on the smooth surface of an old snowpack. In particular in the vicinity of peaks snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be cold. In some localities increase in avalanche danger as a consequence of the strong northwesterly wind. Along the border with France and at elevated altitudes the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent and the danger is greater.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Moist and wet avalanches are possible during the day. Slab avalanches require caution, in the regions exposed to a lot of wind in particular in the afternoon.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation moist and wet avalanches are possible as the day progresses, in particular medium-sized ones.
As a consequence of a moderate to strong westerly wind, clearly visible wind slabs will form in the afternoon in particular adjacent to ridgelines and in pass areas as well as at elevated altitudes. In particular on steep shady slopes avalanches can be released in the various wind slab layers and reach large size in isolated cases. This applies in particular at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions.
Wind slabs can in isolated cases be released, even by a single winter sport participant.

Backcountry touring calls for careful route selection.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
In the early morning the weather will be very mild. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise in the late morning to significant moistening of the snowpack in particular at low and intermediate altitudes.
Afternoon: As a consequence of sharply falling temperatures and the moderate to strong westerly wind, fresh snow drift accumulations will form during the next few days.
In particular intermediate altitudes and steep sunny slopes: The new snow is lying on top of a quite favourable old snowpack. The snowpack is largely stable and its surface has a crust that is strong in many cases.

High altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Wind slabs are lying on the smooth surface of an old snowpack. In particular in the vicinity of peaks snow depths vary greatly, depending on the infuence of the wind.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be cold. In some localities increase in avalanche danger as a consequence of the strong northwesterly wind. Along the border with France and at elevated altitudes the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent and the danger is greater.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Weakly bonded old snow requires caution.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Single winter sport participants can release avalanches in some places. These can in some cases be triggered in near-surface layers. Caution is to be exercised in particular on very steep northwest, north and northeast facing slopes above approximately 2200 m, as well as on very steep west and east facing slopes above approximately 2500 m, in isolated cases also on very steep southeast, south and southwest facing slopes in high Alpine regions. Mostly avalanches are medium-sized.
Avalanche prone locations are to be found in gullies and bowls, and behind abrupt changes in the terrain and adjacent to ridgelines. They are difficult to recognise. Isolated whumpfing sounds can indicate the danger.

Additionally in isolated cases avalanches can be triggered in near-ground layers. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2200 m. Such avalanche prone locations are rare but are barely recognisable. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable.

As a consequence of warming during the day and the solar radiation, the likelihood of moist avalanches being released will increase a little on very steep sunny slopes.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Somewhat older wind slabs are lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack, especially on very steep northwest, north and northeast facing slopes above approximately 2200 m, also on very steep west and east facing slopes above approximately 2500 m, as well as on very steep southeast, south and southwest facing slopes in high Alpine regions.

Towards its base, the snowpack is faceted and weak, especially on shady slopes above approximately 2200 m.

Steep sunny slopes: Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to moistening of the snowpack.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Increase in avalanche danger as a consequence of new snow and wind.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: At elevated altitudes the avalanche prone locations are more prevalent and larger.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: The wind slabs remain in some cases prone to triggering. They must be evaluated with care and prudence. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular at the base of rock walls, and adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls. Avalanches can in many places be released, mostly by large loads. Restraint should be exercised because avalanches can sweep people along and give rise to falls. Backcountry touring calls for meticulous route selection.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: At low and mid-mountain altitudes, the various phases of melting and refreezing have generally favored the formation of a hard, load-bearing surface crust that may become wetter during the day. At higher altitudes, the snowpack is more variable, as these crusts, often load-bearing, alternate with localized accumulations of wind-drifted snow, which are generally clearly visible. Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on shady slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wind slabs and weakly bonded old snow require caution.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Weakly bonded old snow is to be evaluated with care and prudence. These avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular in northwest to north to northeast facing aspects. Caution is to be exercised in particular on very steep shady slopes above the tree line, as well as at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example.
The wind slabs can be released, especially by large additional loads, in particular on west to north to east facing aspects above the tree line. Avalanches can also release deeper layers of the snowpack and reach quite a large size.
As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation individual mostly small moist loose snow slides are possible. This applies on very steep slopes at the base of rock walls.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
As a consequence of a sometimes strong wind from variable directions, sometimes avalanche prone wind slabs formed in the last few days. The fresh and somewhat older wind slabs are lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack.
Distinct weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes. This applies especially above approximately 2000 m.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wind slabs and wet snow represent the main danger.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As a consequence of new snow and wind individual small to medium-sized slab avalanches are possible above approximately 1300 m.
In the vicinity of peaks a dangerous avalanche situation will be encountered in some localities. The more recent wind slabs and can be released easily.
Avalanches can penetrate down to the ground and reach medium size in some cases.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: The weather conditions as the day progresses will give rise to unfavourable bonding of the snowpack in some places.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Weakly bonded old snow is to be evaluated with care and prudence.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Even single winter sport participants can release avalanches as before. These can in some cases be triggered in near-surface layers and reach medium size. Caution is to be exercised in particular on very steep northwest, north and northeast facing slopes above approximately 2200 m, as well as on very steep west and east facing slopes above approximately 2500 m, in isolated cases also on very steep southeast, south and southwest facing slopes in high Alpine regions. Avalanche prone locations are to be found in gullies and bowls, and behind abrupt changes in the terrain and adjacent to ridgelines.
They are difficult to recognise. Isolated whumpfing sounds can indicate the danger.

Additionally in isolated cases avalanches can be triggered in near-ground layers. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2200 m. Such avalanche prone locations are rare but are barely recognisable. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable. Avalanches can in isolated cases reach large size.

As a consequence of warming during the day and the solar radiation, the likelihood of moist avalanches being released will increase a little on very steep sunny slopes.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Somewhat older wind slabs are lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack, especially on very steep northwest, north and northeast facing slopes above approximately 2200 m, also on very steep west and east facing slopes above approximately 2500 m, as well as on very steep southeast, south and southwest facing slopes in high Alpine regions.

Avalanche prone weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack in particular in shady places that are protected from the wind, especially above approximately 2200 m.

Steep sunny slopes: Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to moistening of the snowpack.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Increase in avalanche danger as a consequence of new snow and strong wind.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wind slabs and weakly bonded old snow require caution.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Weakly bonded old snow is to be evaluated with care and prudence. These avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular on steep northwest to north to northeast facing slopes, especially on very steep slopes above the tree line, as well as at transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack, when entering gullies and bowls for example.
The wind slabs can be released, especially by large additional loads, in particular on west to north to east facing aspects above the tree line. Avalanches can also release deeper layers of the snowpack and reach quite a large size.
As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation individual mostly small moist loose snow slides are possible. This applies on very steep slopes at the base of rock walls.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
As a consequence of a sometimes strong wind from variable directions, sometimes avalanche prone wind slabs formed in the last few days. The fresh and somewhat older wind slabs are lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack.
Distinct weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes. This applies especially above approximately 2000 m.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wind slabs and weakly bonded old snow require caution. In addition the danger of dry avalanches will increase towards the evening.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The more recent wind slabs can be released even by a single winter sport participant in all aspects above approximately 2200 m. These are to be found adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls. Mostly the avalanches are medium-sized. The wind slabs are covered with new snow and therefore difficult to recognise.

Weak layers exist in the snowpack on shady slopes. Weak layers in the old snowpack can be released in some places by individual winter sport participants especially here. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable. Avalanches can in very isolated cases be triggered in the old snowpack and reach quite a large size.

As a consequence of warming during the day and the solar radiation, the likelihood of moist avalanches being released will increase a little on very steep sunny slopes.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Distinct weak layers exist deep in the snowpack on shady slopes. Somewhat older wind slabs are lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack, especially on very steep northwest, north and northeast facing slopes above approximately 2200 m.
Over a wide area 5 to 20 cm of snow will fall in the evening above approximately 1500 m. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to slight moistening of the snowpack on sunny slopes.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Stormy weather and new snow: Increase in avalanche danger.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wet snow represents the main danger.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches. Above approximately 1300 m small moist and wet avalanches are possible.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: The snowpack will be in most cases moist.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The snowpack is moist.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: The surface of the snowpack is hardly frozen at all and will already soften in the late morning.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: The snowpack is moist and its surface has a crust that is not capable of bearing a load. This applies in particular on sunny slopes. As a consequence of rising temperatures the snowpack will settle during the next two days. The spring-like weather conditions will give rise to rapid moistening of the snowpack in all aspects.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Low avalanche danger will prevail.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Dry avalanches can be released, in particular by large loads, but they will be small in most cases. Individual avalanche prone locations are to be found on steep shady slopes at high altitude.
As a consequence of warming during the day only isolated moist snow slides and avalanches are possible, but they will be mostly small, in the event of solar radiation in particular on extremely steep slopes.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Shady slopes: Isolated avalanche prone weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.

Sunny slopes: The old snowpack will be moist. The weather conditions will give rise to thorough wetting of the snowpack at intermediate and high altitudes.
